The **Acts of Union 1800** ([Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Achtanna an Aontais 1800*) were parallel acts of the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") and the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") which united the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") (previously in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union")) to create the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). The acts [came into force](/wiki/Came_into_force "Came into force") between 31 December 1800 and 1 January 1801, and the merged [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") had its [first meeting](/wiki/First_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "First Parliament of the United Kingdom") on 22 January 1801.

Provisions of the acts remain in force, with amendments and some Articles repealed, in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Northern_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland"),[[2]](#cite_note-4) but they have been repealed in their entirety in the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland").[[3]](#cite_note-5)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acts_of_Union_1800&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

Two acts were passed in 1800 with the same [long title](/wiki/Long_title "Long title"): *An Act for the Union of Great Britain and Ireland*. The [short title](/wiki/Short_title "Short title") of the act of the British Parliament is **Union with Ireland Act 1800** ([39 & 40 Geo. 3](/wiki/39_%26_40_Geo._3 "39 & 40 Geo. 3"). c. 67), assigned by the [Short Titles Act 1896](/wiki/Short_Titles_Act_1896 "Short Titles Act 1896"). The short title of the act of the Irish Parliament is **Act of Union (Ireland) 1800** ([40 Geo. 3](/wiki/List_of_acts_of_the_Parliament_of_Ireland,_1791%E2%80%931800#40_Geo._3 "List of acts of the Parliament of Ireland, 1791–1800"). c. 38 (I)), assigned by a 1951 act of the [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland"), and hence not effective in the Republic of Ireland, where it was referred to by its long title when repealed in 1962.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acts_of_Union_1800&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

Before these acts, Ireland had been in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with England since 1542, when the [Irish Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") had passed the [Crown of Ireland Act 1542](/wiki/Crown_of_Ireland_Act_1542 "Crown of Ireland Act 1542") ([33 Hen. 8](/wiki/List_of_acts_of_the_Parliament_of_Ireland,_1500%E2%80%931599#33_Hen._8# "List of acts of the Parliament of Ireland, 1500–1599"). c. 1 (I)), proclaiming King [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII") of England to be [King of Ireland](/wiki/King_of_Ireland "King of Ireland"). Since the 12th century, the King of England had been technical overlord of the [Lordship of Ireland](/wiki/Lordship_of_Ireland "Lordship of Ireland"), a papal possession. Both the Kingdoms of Ireland and England later came into personal union with that of [Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") upon the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns") in 1603.

In 1707, the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") and the Kingdom of Scotland were united into a single kingdom: the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). Upon that union, each House of the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") passed a congratulatory address to [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain"), praying her: "May God put it in your royal heart to add greater strength and lustre to your crown, by a still more comprehensive Union".[[4]](#cite_note-6) The Irish Parliament was both before then subject to [certain restrictions](/wiki/Poynings%27_Law_(on_certification_of_acts) "Poynings' Law (on certification of acts)") that made it subordinate to the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") and after then, to the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"); however, Ireland gained effective legislative independence from Great Britain through the [Constitution of 1782](/wiki/Constitution_of_1782 "Constitution of 1782").

By this time access to institutional power in Ireland was restricted to a small minority: the [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish "Anglo-Irish") of the [Protestant Ascendancy](/wiki/Protestant_Ascendancy "Protestant Ascendancy"). Frustration among the Catholic majority at the lack of reform, along with other reasons, eventually led to a [rebellion in 1798](/wiki/1798_Rebellion "1798 Rebellion"), involving a [French invasion of Ireland](/wiki/French_expedition_to_Ireland_(1796) "French expedition to Ireland (1796)") and the seeking of complete independence from Great Britain. This rebellion was crushed with much bloodshed, and the motion for union was motivated at least in part by the belief that the union would alleviate the political rancour that led to the rebellion. The rebellion was felt to have been exacerbated as much by reactionary loyalists as by [United Irishmen](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen") (anti-unionists).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Furthermore, [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation") was being discussed in Great Britain, and fears that a newly enfranchised Catholic majority would drastically change the character of the Irish government and parliament also contributed to a desire from London to merge the Parliaments.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to historian James Stafford, an Enlightenment critique of Empire in Ireland laid the intellectual foundations for the Acts of Union. He writes that Enlightenment thinkers connected "the exclusion of the Irish Kingdom from free participation in imperial and European trade with the exclusion of its Catholic subjects, under the terms of the [penal laws](/wiki/Penal_laws_(Ireland) "Penal laws (Ireland)"), from the benefits of property and political representation". These critiques were used to justify a parliamentary union between Britain and Ireland.[[5]](#cite_note-7)

## Passage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acts_of_Union_1800&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Passage")]

Complementary acts were enacted by the Parliament of Great Britain and the Parliament of Ireland.

The Parliament of Ireland had recently gained a large measure of legislative independence under the [Constitution of 1782](/wiki/Constitution_of_1782 "Constitution of 1782"). Many members of the Irish Parliament jealously guarded that autonomy (notably [Henry Grattan](/wiki/Henry_Grattan "Henry Grattan")), and a motion for union was legally rejected in 1799. Only [Anglicans](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") were permitted to become members of the Parliament of Ireland though the great majority of the Irish population were [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), with many [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterians "Presbyterians") in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"). Under the [Roman Catholic Relief Act 1793](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1793 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1793"), Roman Catholics regained the right to vote if they owned or rented property worth £2 annually. Wealthy Catholics were strongly in favour of union in the hope for rapid [religious emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation") and the right to sit as MPs, which would only come to pass under the [Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1829 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829").

From the perspective of Great Britain's elites, the union was desirable because of the uncertainty that followed the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") of 1789 and the [Irish Rebellion of 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798"). If Ireland adopted Catholic emancipation willingly or not, a Roman Catholic Parliament could break away from Britain and ally with the French, but the same measure within the United Kingdom would exclude that possibility. Also, in creating a regency during King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III")'s "madness", the Irish and British Parliaments gave the Prince Regent different powers. These considerations led Great Britain to decide to attempt the merger of both kingdoms and Parliaments.

The final passage of the Act in the [Irish House of Commons](/wiki/Irish_House_of_Commons "Irish House of Commons") turned on an about 16% relative majority, garnering 58% of the votes, and similar in the [Irish House of Lords](/wiki/Irish_House_of_Lords "Irish House of Lords"), in part per contemporary accounts through bribery with the awarding of [peerages](/wiki/Peerage "Peerage") and [honours](/wiki/Honour "Honour") to critics to get votes.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard199428-8) The first attempt had been defeated in the Irish House of Commons by 109 votes to 104, but the second vote in 1800 passed by 158 to 115.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWard199428-8)

## Provisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acts_of_Union_1800&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Provisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Order_in_Council%2C_5th_November_1800_%E2%80%93_illustrations_of_the_new_arms%2C_flag%2C_and_standard_of_the_United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland_%28PC_2-157%29.jpg/250px-Order_in_Council%2C_5th_November_1800_%E2%80%93_illustrations_of_the_new_arms%2C_flag%2C_and_standard_of_the_United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland_%28PC_2-157%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Order_in_Council,_5th_November_1800_%E2%80%93_illustrations_of_the_new_arms,_flag,_and_standard_of_the_United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland_(PC_2-157).jpg)

[Order in Council](/wiki/Order_in_Council "Order in Council") of November 1800, illustrating the new arms, flag, and royal standard of the United Kingdom

The Acts of Union were two complementary Acts, namely:

* The Union with Ireland Act 1800 (39 & 40 Geo. 3 c. 67),[[7]](#cite_note-9) an Act of the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), and
* The Act of Union (Ireland) 1800 (40 Geo. 3 c. 38),[[8]](#cite_note-10) an Act of the [Parliament of Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland").

They were passed on 2 July 1800 and 1 August 1800 respectively, and came into force on 1 January 1801. They ratified eight articles which had been previously agreed by the British and Irish parliaments:

* Articles I–IV dealt with the political aspects of the Union. It created a [united parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom").
  + In the House of Lords, the existing members of the Parliament of Great Britain were joined by, as [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual"), four bishops of the [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland"), rotating among the dioceses in each session and as [Lords Temporal](/wiki/Lords_Temporal "Lords Temporal") 28 [Irish representative peers](/wiki/List_of_Irish_representative_peers "List of Irish representative peers") elected for life by the [Peerage of Ireland](/wiki/Peerage_of_Ireland "Peerage of Ireland").
  + The House of Commons was to include the pre-union representation from Great Britain and 100 members from Ireland.

:   :   See also: [List of United Kingdom parliamentary constituencies in Ireland 1801–1885](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_parliamentary_constituencies_in_Ireland_1801%E2%80%931885 "List of United Kingdom parliamentary constituencies in Ireland 1801–1885")

* Article V united the established [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and Church of Ireland into "one Protestant Episcopal Church, to be called, The United Church of England and Ireland"; but also confirmed the independence of the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland").
* Article VI created a [customs union](/wiki/Customs_union "Customs union"), with the exception that customs duties on certain British and Irish goods passing between the two countries would remain for 10 years (a consequence of having trade depressed by the ongoing war with revolutionary France). The High Court of Northern Ireland ruled that parts of this Article as it applied to the UK were "[impliedly repealed](/wiki/Implied_repeal "Implied repeal")" by the passage of the [European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2020](/wiki/European_Union_(Withdrawal_Agreement)_Act_2020 "European Union (Withdrawal Agreement) Act 2020").[[9]](#cite_note-11) This decision was upheld on appeal by the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom").[[10]](#cite_note-12)[[11]](#cite_note-13)
* Article VII stated that Ireland would have to contribute two-seventeenths towards the expenditure of the United Kingdom. The figure was a ratio of Irish to British foreign trade.
* Article VIII formalised the legal and judicial aspects of the Union.

Part of the appeal of the Union for many Irish Catholics was the promise of [Catholic emancipation](/wiki/Catholic_emancipation "Catholic emancipation"), allowing [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") MPs, who had not been permitted to sit in the Irish Parliament, to sit in the United Kingdom Parliament. This was however blocked by King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") who argued that emancipating Roman Catholics would breach his [Coronation Oath](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_British_monarch#Recognition_and_oath "Coronation of the British monarch"), and was not realised until the [Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Relief_Act_1829 "Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829").

The traditionally separate [Irish Army](/wiki/Irish_Army_(Kingdom_of_Ireland) "Irish Army (Kingdom of Ireland)"), which had been funded by the Irish Parliament, was merged into the larger [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army").

### The first parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acts_of_Union_1800&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The first parliament")]

Main article: [First Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/First_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "First Parliament of the United Kingdom")

In the first Parliament of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, the members of the House of Commons were not elected afresh. By royal proclamation authorised by the Act, all the members of the last House of Commons from Great Britain took seats in the new House, and from Ireland [100 members were chosen](/wiki/Members_of_the_1st_UK_Parliament_from_Ireland "Members of the 1st UK Parliament from Ireland") from the last Irish House of Commons: two members from each of the 32 counties and the two largest boroughs, and one from each of the next 31 largest boroughs and from [Dublin University](/wiki/Dublin_University_(constituency) "Dublin University (constituency)"), chosen by lot. The other 84 Irish parliamentary boroughs were disfranchised; all were [pocket boroughs](/wiki/Pocket_borough "Pocket borough"), whose patrons received £15,000 compensation for the loss of what was considered their property.

[![Second Union Flag](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ae/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom.svg)

Main article: [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack")

The Union Flag, created as a consequence of the union of the Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland in 1800, still remains the [flag of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_Kingdom "Flag of the United Kingdom"). Called the Union Jack, it combined the flags of [St George's Cross](/wiki/St_George%27s_Cross "St George's Cross") (which was deemed to include [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales")) and the [St Andrew's Saltire](/wiki/Flag_of_Scotland "Flag of Scotland") of Scotland with the [St Patrick's Saltire](/wiki/St_Patrick%27s_Saltire "St Patrick's Saltire") to represent Ireland.

At the same time, a new Royal Title was adopted ('GEORGE the THIRD by the Grace of God of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland King, Defender of the Faith'), and a new shield of arms.[[12]](#cite_note-14) In adopting these, the moribund [English claims to the French throne](/wiki/English_claims_to_the_French_throne "English claims to the French throne") were not continued: the title 'King of France' was abandoned and the [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis") were removed from the [Royal Standard of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Royal_Standard_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal Standard of the United Kingdom") for the first time since the Middle Ages.
